answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: These considered three alternative futures: • Orderly – 1.5° C ... • Too Little Too Late – >2° C ... • Hothouse– >3° C
answers.location_hint: p.9 (Strategy section) and Appendix A, p.17
answers.notes: The three scenarios are also summarised in a table in Appendix A (p.17) with the headings: Orderly 1.5°C, Too Little Too Late >2°C, Hothouse >3°C.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: (1) Orderly scenario: Global +1.4°C (min 1.0, max 1.8), Domestic (NZ) +0.7°C (min 0.4, max 1.3); (2) Too Little Too Late scenario: Global +2.7°C (min 2.1, max 3.5), Domestic +1.4°C (min 0.7, max 2.2); (3) Hothouse scenario: Global +4.4°C (min 3.3, max 5.7), Domestic +3.0°C (min 2.0, max 4.6). The scenarios are labelled 1.5°C, >2°C, and >3°C respectively. No explicit temperature overshoot language is used.
answers.evidence: Average temperature increase by 2100: • Domestic: +0.7°C by 2100 (min 0.4, max 1.3) • Global: +1.4° C by 2100 (min 1.0, max 1.8) ... Average temperature increase by 2100: • Domestic: +1.4°C by 2100 (min 0.7, max 2.2) • Global: +2.7° C by 2100 (min 2.1, max 3.5) ... Average temperature increase by 2100: • Domestic: +3.0°C by 2100 (min 2.0, max 4.6) • Global: +4.4° C by 2100 (min 3.3, max 5.7)
answers.location_hint: Appendix A, p.17 (scenario table, Environmental row)
answers.notes: Note that the scenario labels (1.5°C, >2°C, >3°C) differ from the modelled central estimates (1.4°C, 2.7°C, 4.4°C globally). The 1.5°C scenario is noted as having declining plausibility given observed 2024 temperatures.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Yes. The scenario narratives were developed by EY and the Financial Services Council (FSC), through the FSC's Climate Working Group. Multiple underlying data sources are also named: IPCC (global socio-economic parameters), NGFS (global energy/emission pathways), IEA (global energy pathways), NIWA (NZ-specific climate parameters), and CCC/Climate Change Commission (NZ-specific transition pathways).
answers.evidence: Chubb Life was a member of the FSC's Climate Working Group, which was involved in the development of the climate scenario narratives for the financial services sector. ... 6 Climate Scenario Narratives for the Financial Services Sector, EY and FSC, page 29
answers.location_hint: Appendix A, p.17; footnotes on p.9
answers.notes: The primary scenario narrative provider is EY and FSC (Financial Services Council). The underlying scientific/policy data providers are IPCC, NGFS, IEA, NIWA, and CCC.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: Global climate and socio-economic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP2-4.5 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 ... New Zealand-specific climate parameters: NIWA RCP2.6 / NIWA RCP4.5 / NIWA RCP 8.5
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP identifiers (IPCC SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5) and RCP identifiers (NIWA RCP2.6, RCP4.5, RCP8.5) within the same scenario framework. SSPs and RCPs are from different generation scenario families (CMIP6 vs CMIP5), used together across global and NZ-specific parameters respectively.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: Chubb Life was a member of the FSC's Climate Working Group, which was involved in the development of the climate scenario narratives for the financial services sector. The use of sectoral scenarios allows consumers to more easily compare disclosures. ... In addition to the industry scenarios, our Risk and Compliance team led internal research to deepen our understanding of our alternative futures, including the impacts specific to New Zealand in terms of both physical and transitional impacts. We also considered factors specific to Chubb Life, including our value chain (as described above) and the distribution of our customers across New Zealand.
answers.location_hint: Appendix A, p.17; Strategy section, p.9
answers.notes: The scenarios were developed specifically for the New Zealand financial services sector (via the FSC/EY process), incorporating NZ-specific climate parameters (NIWA RCPs) and NZ-specific transition pathways (CCC). Chubb Life further adapted them with internal research specific to their business and NZ context. The word "tailored" is not used explicitly, but the process clearly goes beyond off-the-shelf use.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: The process to determine the scenario narratives followed five stages: 1. Engagement of stakeholders, including the establishment of the working group and the Steering Committee; 2. Setting the focal question, which included determining the time horizons and key categories of climate-related risk; 3. Identifying driving forces, utilizing STEEP analysis... 4. Selected the scenarios and identified risks, opportunities and their pathways. Work was undertaken with the members to identify the key climate-related risks, and the impacts of these risks under different scenarios; and 5. Draft and revising the narratives.
answers.notes: The scenario development process explicitly involved stakeholder engagement, a working group, and a Steering Committee. Chubb Life was a member of the FSC's Climate Working Group that co-developed the narratives. This constitutes participatory/co-produced scenario development.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit standalone baseline/reference scenario is described against which the three climate scenarios are compared. However, a base year of FY23 is used as the starting point for scenario analysis, and the Hothouse scenario incorporates a "Current Policy Reference" emissions pathway from the CCC.
answers.evidence: For each scenario we assessed potential short-term (1-3 years), medium term (5-10 years), and long term (30+ years) impacts (from a baseline year of FY23). ... New Zealand-specific transition pathway parameters: CCC 'Current Policy Reference' Emissions pathway
answers.notes: There is no dedicated "business as usual" or "reference" scenario explicitly set up as a comparator. The CCC "Current Policy Reference" is a parameter within the Hothouse scenario, not a standalone baseline. FY23 is used as a base year for time horizon calculations.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: Global: NGFS Net Zero by 2050 25.9 BtCO2e by 2050, -294.82 MtCO2e by 2050 using GCAM5.3+ (NGFS) ... GCAM Global Change Analysis Model
answers.location_hint: Appendix A, p.17 (emissions pathway row and abbreviations table)
answers.notes: GCAM5.3+ is the only named model. It is used by NGFS for emissions pathway calculations. No other IAMs, climate models, or macroeconomic models are explicitly named.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Adverse weather events: Flooding and other acute physical risks could lead to increased insurance costs for Chubb Life and potential costs for retreat/adaptation, even where Chubb Life facilities are not directly impacted. ... GDP (GDP % change due to chronic physical risk, acute impacts are excluded from this figure and would further negatively impact GDP)
answers.location_hint: p.11 (physical risks table); Appendix A, p.18 (Economic row)
answers.notes: Acute physical risks (flooding, landslides, extreme weather events) are explicitly considered in the scenario analysis. The GDP table notes that acute impacts are excluded from the GDP figures shown but acknowledges they "would further negatively impact GDP." Damage/loss estimates from acute events are discussed qualitatively but not fully quantified.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: GHG emissions pathway (domestic and global, MtCO2e)
percent of renewable electricity of total electricity produced (domestic and global)
percent of renewable energy of total energy produced (domestic and global)
answers.evidence: Carbon price: • Domestic ETS: NZ$140 in 2030, NZ$250 in 2050 • Global: US$124 in 2030, US$400 in 2050 ... Global population: 8 billion by 2030, 8.5 billion by 2050 ... Percent of renewable electricity of total electricity produced ... NZ GDP: NZ$330 billion (-0.5%) in 2030 ... Physical risks arising from climate change can also be chronic, due to longer term shifts in climate patterns. These include persistent high temperatures, rising sea levels, and changes in rainfall.
answers.location_hint: Appendix A, pp.17–18 (scenario table); p.11 (physical risks section)
answers.notes: Variables are drawn from the scenario narrative table (Policy, Social, Technological, Economic rows) and the physical risks narrative section.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: Global climate and socio-economic parameters: IPCC SSP1-1.9 / IPCC SSP2-4.5 / IPCC SSP5-8.5 ... New Zealand-specific climate parameters: NIWA RCP2.6 / NIWA RCP4.5 / NIWA RCP 8.5 ... New Zealand-specific transition pathway parameters: CCC 'Tailwinds' / CCC 'Headwinds' / CCC 'Current Policy Reference'
answers.notes: The scenarios explicitly combine global-level parameters (IPCC SSPs, NGFS, IEA) with New Zealand-specific (regional/local) parameters from NIWA (climate) and CCC (transition pathways). Both domestic and global figures are provided throughout the scenario table.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 1–3 years; Medium-term: 5–10 years; Long-term: 30+ years. All measured from a base year of FY23. Rationale is provided: short-to-medium term aligns with strategic planning horizons (~5 years); long-term is not linked to strategic planning but aligns with the long-term nature of life insurance policies written.
answers.evidence: For each scenario we assessed potential short-term (1-3 years), medium term (5-10 years), and long term (30+ years) impacts (from a baseline year of FY23). The short to medium term time frames align with our strategic planning horizons (of approximately five years). The long-term timeframe is not linked to our strategic planning or capital deployment, but provides an interesting opportunity for us to consider risks and opportunities in a timeframe that aligns with the long-term nature of the life insurance policies we write.
answers.notes: Rationale is explicitly provided for all three time horizons.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: The sector narratives are supported by robust rationale for their inclusion. The Orderly and Hothouse scenarios are commonly used in relevant industries, and have been used in other New Zealand sectoral analyses. The Too Little Too Late scenario is not commonly used, but is considered more realistic than the "Disorderly" scenario. In order to further satisfy ourselves that the scenarios were relevant and appropriate, we reviewed the narratives against the scenario quality check factors set out in the XRB's Staff Guidance on Scenario development, as well as the express requirements of the Climate Standards.
answers.notes: Justification is explicitly provided: industry common use, NZ sectoral precedent, realism compared to alternatives, and validation against XRB Staff Guidance and Climate Standards requirements. The Orderly scenario is also noted as mandatory under CS1 despite declining plausibility (footnote 7, p.9).
